nl_task_title: Uploading Documents with Secure Storage 0/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Uploading Documents with Secure Storage
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, Database

Interactions:

User Action: The user uploads a document (e.g., an ID or contract) through the mobile app.
Mobile App: Sends the document along with the session token to the BFF.
BFF Validation:
Validates the session token with Azure AD.
Checks user permissions to ensure they are authorized to upload documents.
Storage Process:
The BFF saves metadata about the document (e.g., file name, size, upload timestamp) in the database.
The actual document is securely stored in cloud storage (e.g., Azure Blob Storage).
Response:
On successful upload, the BFF returns a confirmation to the app.
If the user is unauthorized or the file exceeds size limits, an appropriate error is returned.
input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

Purpose: Uploading Documents with Secure Storage
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, Database

Interactions:

User Action: The user uploads a document (e.g., an ID or contract) through the mobile app.
Mobile App: Sends the document along with the session token to the BFF.
BFF Validation:
Validates the session token with Azure AD.
Checks user permissions to ensure they are authorized to upload documents.
Storage Process:
The BFF saves metadata about the document (e.g., file name, size, upload timestamp) in the database.
The actual document is securely stored in cloud storage (e.g., Azure Blob Storage).
Response:
On successful upload, the BFF returns a confirmation to the app.
If the user is unauthorized or the file exceeds size limits, an appropriate error is returned.
expected_output:
sequenceDiagram
participant User
participant MobileApp
participant BackendForFrontend
participant AzureAD
participant Database
participant AzureBlobStorage

Note over User: Upload Document
User->>MobileApp: Upload document
MobileApp->>BackendForFrontend: Send document (with session token)
activate BackendForFrontend
BackendForFrontend->>AzureAD: Validate session token
deactivate BackendForFrontend
activate AzureAD
AzureAD-->>BackendForFrontend: Token validated
deactivate AzureAD
activate BackendForFrontend
BackendForFrontend->>AzureAD: Check user permissions
deactivate BackendForFrontend
activate AzureAD
AzureAD-->>BackendForFrontend: Permissions valid
deactivate AzureAD
activate BackendForFrontend

alt Invalid token, insufficient permissions, or file too large
  BackendForFrontend-->>MobileApp: Error message
else Upload successful
  BackendForFrontend->>Database: Save document metadata
  activate Database
  Database-->>BackendForFrontend: Metadata saved
  deactivate Database

  BackendForFrontend->>AzureBlobStorage: Store document
  activate AzureBlobStorage
  AzureBlobStorage-->>BackendForFrontend: Document stored
  deactivate AzureBlobStorage

  BackendForFrontend-->>MobileApp: Confirmation
  
end
deactivate BackendForFrontend
llm_response:
```sequenceDiagram
participant User
participant MobileApp
participant BFF
participant AzureAD
participant Database

User->>MobileApp: Upload document
activate MobileApp
MobileApp->>BFF: POST /documents(sessionToken, file)
activate BFF
BFF->>AzureAD: POST /validateToken(sessionToken)
activate AzureAD
alt Token valid
    AzureAD-->>BFF: 200 OK (userId, permissions)
    deactivate AzureAD
    BFF->>BFF: Check upload permissions
    alt User authorized
        BFF->>BFF: Validate file size
        alt File size within limit
            BFF->>Database: INSERT document_metadata(name, size, timestamp, userId)
            activate Database
            Database-->>BFF: 201 Created (documentId)
            deactivate Database
            Note over BFF: Store actual file in Azure Blob Storage
            BFF-->>MobileApp: 201 Created (documentId, confirmation)
        else File size exceeds limit
            BFF-->>MobileApp: 413 Payload Too Large
        end
    else User unauthorized
        BFF-->>MobileApp: 403 Forbidden
    end
else Token invalid
    AzureAD-->>BFF: 401 Unauthorized
    deactivate AzureAD
    BFF-->>MobileApp: 401 Unauthorized
end
deactivate BFF
deactivate MobileApp
```
llm_judge_syntax_response: 1.000; The diagram uses valid MermaidJS sequenceDiagram syntax. All lifelines are declared with `participant Name` at the top. Message arrows (`->>` and `-->>`), notes, and self-calls are syntactically correct. Every `activate` has a corresponding `deactivate` for MobileApp, BFF, AzureAD, and Database, with no unbalanced activations. The `alt`/`else`/`end` blocks are properly formed and correctly nested: an outer `alt Token valid / else Token invalid / end`, with inner `alt User authorized / else User unauthorized / end` and `alt File size within limit / else File size exceeds limit / end`. Overall, it is structurally and syntactically correct MermaidJS.
llm_judge_mermaid_only_response: 0.950; The response is fully wrapped in a Markdown code block starting with ```sequenceDiagram and contains only Mermaid sequence diagram syntax with no extra prose. The structure (participants, messages, alt/else/end, Note) is valid and should be parsable/renderable by Mermaid. Minor stylistic differences from the reference (e.g., inline comment-like note about Azure Blob Storage rather than an explicit participant) do not affect validity.
llm_judge_logic_response: 0.900; Syntax is valid MermaidJS, with correct use of participants, activation/deactivation, alt/else blocks, and notes. Every request has a corresponding response: MobileApp↔BFF, BFF↔AzureAD (both valid and invalid token), BFF↔Database. Alternate flows are clearly represented and nested correctly: token valid/invalid; within valid, authorized/unauthorized; within authorized, file size within limit/exceeds limit. All described error cases (unauthorized user, invalid token, file too large) and the success path are covered. The only notable omission versus the specification/reference is that Azure Blob Storage is not modeled as a separate participant and the actual file storage is only represented as a note rather than an explicit interaction, so the storage process is less explicit than requested. Otherwise, logic and flow completeness are strong.
llm_judge_completeness_response: 0.900; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and participants are correctly ordered (User → MobileApp → BFF → AzureAD → Database). All key interactions are present: user upload, app sending document and token to BFF, token validation with Azure AD, permission check, file size validation, metadata save to DB, and final responses. Requests have corresponding responses, and alt blocks cover token validity, authorization, and file size error paths with appropriate HTTP codes. Minor deviations: Azure Blob Storage is only mentioned in a note rather than modeled as an explicit participant with request/response, and the BFF is self-calling for permission/size checks instead of involving Azure AD for permissions as in the reference, but these are acceptable design choices. Overall coverage of described behavior and error flows is very good, with only slight under-specification of the storage interaction.
llm_judge_activation_handling_response: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and activations are mostly used correctly: MobileApp and BFF are activated when they receive messages and deactivated at the end, AzureAD and Database are activated on incoming calls and deactivated after their responses. Every `activate` has a matching `deactivate`, and there are no `deactivate` calls without a prior `activate`. One minor issue is that AzureAD is deactivated inside both the `alt Token valid` and `else Token invalid` branches, even though it was only activated once before the `alt`; this is acceptable but slightly redundant and could be simplified by deactivating once after the `alt`. Overall, the activation usage clearly improves readability and traceability of the flow.
llm_judge_error_and_status_tracking_resp: 0.900; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and uses participants, activation, deactivation, alt/else/end, and notes correctly. Status updates are explicit via HTTP-style responses (200 OK, 201 Created, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 413 Payload Too Large) and clear messages back to the MobileApp. Success and failure flows are clearly separated with nested `alt` blocks: token valid/invalid, then authorized/unauthorized, then file size within limit/exceeds limit, with distinct responses for each error type. Error handling is detailed and specific, covering invalid token, lack of permissions, and file size violations, each returning appropriate status codes. The diagram tracks the state of key entities (MobileApp, BFF, AzureAD, Database) through activations and deactivations, though Azure Blob Storage is only mentioned in a note rather than as a participant, so the storage step’s state is less explicitly modeled than in the reference; otherwise, handling of errors and status updates is very clear.

participant MobileApp

nl_task_title: Real-Time Notifications for Workflow Updates 1/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Real-Time Notifications for Workflow Updates
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, Database

Interactions:
User Action: A technician updates a workflow task (e.g., marking a job as completed) from their mobile app.
Mobile App: Sends the task update to the BFF with the session token.
BFF Validation:
Validates the session token with Azure AD.
Checks the user’s role and permissions for the specific workflow task.
Database Update:
The BFF updates the workflow task status in the database.
It triggers an event to notify stakeholders (e.g., a dispatcher or customer).
Real-Time Notification:
The BFF uses a notification service (e.g., Azure Notification Hubs or Firebase) to send a real-time push notification to the mobile app of all relevant stakeholders.
Notifications may include details like the updated status, timestamp, and who updated the task.
Response:
The app displays the updated workflow details and confirms the task status change.
If the update fails (e.g., invalid permissions), an error message is sent to the app.

sequenceDiagram
participant U as User
participant MA as Mobile App
participant BFF as Backend for Frontend
participant AAD as Azure AD
participant DB as Database
participant NH as Notification Hub

Note over U: Update Workflow Task
U->>MA: Update task (e.g., mark as completed)
MA->>BFF: Send task update (with session token)
activate BFF
BFF->>AAD: Validate session token
activate AAD
AAD-->>BFF: Token validated
deactivate AAD
BFF->>AAD: Check user permissions
activate AAD
AAD-->>BFF: Permissions valid
deactivate AAD

alt Invalid token or insufficient permissions
  BFF-->>MA: Error message
else Valid update
  BFF->>DB: Update task status
  activate DB
  DB-->>BFF: Status updated
  deactivate DB
  BFF->>NH: Trigger notification with task details

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as User
    participant Mobile as Mobile App
    participant BFF as BFF
    participant AzureAD as Azure AD
    participant DB as Database
    participant Notification as Notification Service

    User->>Mobile: Update workflow task (mark as completed)
    activate Mobile
    Mobile->>BFF: POST /workflow/task/update (taskId, status, sessionToken)
    activate BFF
    
    BFF->>AzureAD: POST /validateToken (sessionToken)
    activate AzureAD
    alt Token Valid
        AzureAD-->>BFF: 200 OK (userInfo)
        deactivate AzureAD
        
        BFF->>AzureAD: GET /user/permissions (userId, taskId)
        activate AzureAD
        alt Has Permission
            AzureAD-->>BFF: 200 OK (permissions)
            deactivate AzureAD
            
            BFF->>DB: PUT /workflow/task (taskId, status, userId)
            activate DB
            alt Update Successful
                DB-->>BFF: 200 OK (updatedTask)
                deactivate DB
                
                BFF->>DB: GET /workflow/stakeholders (taskId)
                activate DB
                DB-->>BFF: 200 OK (stakeholderList)
                deactivate DB
                
                BFF->>Notification: POST /sendNotification (stakeholderList, taskUpdate)
                activate Notification
                Notification-->>BFF: 200 OK (notificationId)
                deactivate Notification
                
                BFF-->>Mobile: 200 OK (taskUpdated)
                deactivate BFF
                Mobile-->>User: Display updated workflow details
                deactivate Mobile
                
                Notification->>Mobile: Push notification (task status, timestamp, updatedBy)
                activate Mobile
                Mobile-->>User: Show real-time notification
                deactivate Mobile
            else Update Failed
                DB-->>BFF: 500 Error (update failed)
                deactivate DB
                BFF-->>Mobile: 500 Error (task update failed)
                deactivate BFF
                Mobile-->>User: Show error message
                deactivate Mobile
            end
        else No Permission
            AzureAD-->>BFF: 403 Forbidden
            deactivate AzureAD
            BFF-->>Mobile: 403 Error (insufficient permissions)
            deactivate BFF
            Mobile-->>User: Show permission denied error
            deactivate Mobile
        end
    else Token Invalid
        AzureAD-->>BFF: 401 Unauthorized
        deactivate AzureAD
        BFF-->>Mobile: 401 Error (invalid session)
        deactivate BFF
        Mobile-->>User: Show login required message
        deactivate Mobile
    end
```

nl_task_title: Real-Time Notifications for Workflow Updates 1/III
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Real-Time Notifications for Workflow Updates
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, Database

Interactions:
User Action: A technician updates a workflow task (e.g., marking a job as completed) from their mobile app.
Mobile App: Sends the task update to the BFF with the session token.
BFF Validation:
Validates the session token with Azure AD.
Checks the user’s role and permissions for the specific workflow task.
Database Update:
The BFF updates the workflow task status in the database.
It triggers an event to notify stakeholders (e.g., a dispatcher or customer).
Real-Time Notification:
The BFF uses a notification service (e.g., Azure Notification Hubs or Firebase) to send a real-time push notification to the mobile app of all relevant stakeholders.
Notifications may include details like the updated status, timestamp, and who updated the task.
Response:
The app displays the updated workflow details and confirms the task status change.
If the update fails (e.g., invalid permissions), an error message is sent to the app.

sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:

- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.

- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

nl_task_title: OAuth login flow for a mobile application 2/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: OAuth login flow for a mobile application
Main components: User, Mobile app, BFF, Azure AD, Database
Interactions:
User enters the credentials from the mobile application.
Mobile application redirects the user to Azure AD's authentication page.
User enters credentials on Azure AD's login page.
Azure AD validates the credentials.
If credentials are valid, Azure AD returns an authorization code to the mobile application (or BFF).
If credentials are incorrect, Azure AD returns an error response (invalid_grant), and the mobile app displays an error message.
If the account is locked due to multiple failed attempts, Azure AD returns an account_locked error, prompting the user to reset their password.
Mobile application sends the authorization code to BFF.
BFF exchanges the authorization code with Azure AD for an access token (and optionally a refresh token).
If the authorization code is expired or invalid, Azure AD returns invalid_grant, and the mobile app must restart the login process.
BFF stores the refresh token (if applicable) in the database.
BFF sends the access token back to the mobile application.
Mobile application uses the access token to access protected APIs.
If the access token is expired or revoked, the API returns invalid_token, and the mobile app must refresh it.
If the token lacks required scopes, the API returns insufficient_scope, and the mobile app prompts the user for additional permissions.
Mobile application requests a new access token using the refresh token when needed.
If the refresh token is expired or revoked, Azure AD returns refresh_token_expired, forcing a re-login.

deactivate SR```

Purpose: OAuth login flow for a mobile application
Main components: User, Mobile app, BFF, Azure AD, Database
Interactions:
User enters the credentials from the mobile application.
Mobile application redirects the user to Azure AD's authentication page.
User enters credentials on Azure AD's login page.
Azure AD validates the credentials.
If credentials are valid, Azure AD returns an authorization code to the mobile application (or BFF).
If credentials are incorrect, Azure AD returns an error response (invalid_grant), and the mobile app displays an error message.
If the account is locked due to multiple failed attempts, Azure AD returns an account_locked error, prompting the user to reset their password.
Mobile application sends the authorization code to BFF.
BFF exchanges the authorization code with Azure AD for an access token (and optionally a refresh token).
If the authorization code is expired or invalid, Azure AD returns invalid_grant, and the mobile app must restart the login process.
BFF stores the refresh token (if applicable) in the database.
BFF sends the access token back to the mobile application.
Mobile application uses the access token to access protected APIs.
If the access token is expired or revoked, the API returns invalid_token, and the mobile app must refresh it.
If the token lacks required scopes, the API returns insufficient_scope, and the mobile app prompts the user for additional permissions.
Mobile application requests a new access token using the refresh token when needed.
If the refresh token is expired or revoked, Azure AD returns refresh_token_expired, forcing a re-login.

nl_task_title: Send a notification to an end user via the Notification Server 3/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Send a notification to an end user via the Notification Server.
Main Components: End User, Mobile App, BFF (Backend for Frontend), Notification Server, APNS/FCM, SOR (System of Record)
Interactions:
1. Event Trigger: A specific event occurs (e.g., order update, message received) in SOR that requires notifying the end user.
2. BFF Notification Request: The BFF sends a request to the Notification Server, including details such as the target user/device, notification type, and message content.
3. Notification Delivery: The Notification Server pushes the notification to the respective mobile app using push notification service (e.g., APNs for iOS, FCM for Android).
4. Mobile App Handling: The Mobile App receives the notification, processes it (e.g., displaying an alert, updating UI)
5. User Interaction: The end user interacts with the notification (e.g., taps to open the app, dismisses it, or takes an action).

nl_task_title: Send a notification to an end user via the Notification Server 3/IV
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Send a notification to an end user via the Notification Server.
Main Components: End User, Mobile App, BFF (Backend for Frontend), Notification Server, APNS/FCM, SOR (System of Record)
Interactions:
1. Event Trigger: A specific event occurs (e.g., order update, message received) in SOR that requires notifying the end user.
2. BFF Notification Request: The BFF sends a request to the Notification Server, including details such as the target user/device, notification type, and message content.
3. Notification Delivery: The Notification Server pushes the notification to the respective mobile app using push notification service (e.g., APNs for iOS, FCM for Android).
4. Mobile App Handling: The Mobile App receives the notification, processes it (e.g., displaying an alert, updating UI)
5. User Interaction: The end user interacts with the notification (e.g., taps to open the app, dismisses it, or takes an action).
input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

nl_task_title: Read water meter 4/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Read water meter
Main components: User, Mobile app, BFF, MDM, Database
Interactions:
- User creates login to the app
- Scans the meter and send Meter ID to MDM through BFF
- if Meter available 
  - MDM responds Water Address, Property Type, Past Meter reads
  - User validates the data
  - if address is not valid
    - if address available in map
      - user selects address from Map
    - else
      - user collects data required to create Unique Address ID
  - User colelcts the meter read
  - User submits the form. Formdata is sent to BFF for further processing.
- else
  - MDM responds as No Meter

input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.

end

Similarly, Mobile is activated at the start and only deactivated in the "Token valid" branch; in the invalid-token path Mobile never deactivates. Error handling is explicit for the invalid-token case with a clear 401 Unauthorized response and a UI reaction (show login screen). Success and failure flows are clearly separated via the `alt Token invalid / else Token valid` block, and the success path includes both fetching and updating the profile with 200 OK status codes. However, the update-profile flow has no explicit error branch (e.g., SOR failure or validation error), so not all plausible error cases are modeled. State changes (profile fetched, profile updated, user shown success/login) are implied and reasonably tracked, but could be clearer with more explicit status notes or additional alt blocks for update failures. nl_task_title: User registration and account creation process 6/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: User registration and account creation process
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Database, Email Service
Interactions:
1. User Registration: The user fills out the registration form on the mobile app.
2. Data Submission: The mobile app sends the registration data to the BFF.
3. Data Validation: The BFF validates the registration data (e.g., checks for existing users, valid email format).
4. Database Insertion: The BFF inserts the validated data into the database, creating a new user account.
5. Email Verification: The BFF sends a verification email to the user's email address using the Email Service.
6. Email Confirmation: The user clicks the verification link in the email, which redirects to the mobile app.
7. Account Activation: The mobile app sends a confirmation to the BFF, which activates the user's account in the database.
8. Success Notification: The mobile app displays a success message to the user, indicating that the account has been created and verified.

nl_task_title: User registration and account creation process 6/II
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: User registration and account creation process
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Database, Email Service
Interactions:
1. User Registration: The user fills out the registration form on the mobile app.
2. Data Submission: The mobile app sends the registration data to the BFF.
3. Data Validation: The BFF validates the registration data (e.g., checks for existing users, valid email format).
4. Database Insertion: The BFF inserts the validated data into the database, creating a new user account.
5. Email Verification: The BFF sends a verification email to the user's email address using the Email Service.
6. Email Confirmation: The user clicks the verification link in the email, which redirects to the mobile app.
7. Account Activation: The mobile app sends a confirmation to the BFF, which activates the user's account in the database.
8. Success Notification: The mobile app displays a success message to the user, indicating that the account has been created and verified.
input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

sequenceDiagram
participant User
participant MobileApp
participant BackendForFrontend
participant Database
participant EmailService

Note over User: User Registration
User->>MobileApp: Fill out registration form
MobileApp->>BackendForFrontend: Send registration data
activate BackendForFrontend
BackendForFrontend->>BackendForFrontend: Validate registration data 
Note over BackendForFrontend: check for existing users, valid email format, etc
alt Validation Successful
    BackendForFrontend->>Database: Insert validated data
    deactivate BackendForFrontend
    activate Database
    Database-->>BackendForFrontend: Confirm data insertion
    deactivate Database
    activate BackendForFrontend
    BackendForFrontend->>EmailService: Send verification email
    activate EmailService
    EmailService-->>User: Verification email sent
    deactivate EmailService
    deactivate BackendForFrontend
    User->>MobileApp: Click verification link
    MobileApp->>BackendForFrontend: Send confirmation
    activate BackendForFrontend
    BackendForFrontend->>Database: Activate user account
    activate Database
    Database-->>BackendForFrontend: Confirm account activation
    deactivate Database
    BackendForFrontend-->>MobileApp: Confirm account activation
    deactivate BackendForFrontend
    MobileApp->>User: Display success message
else Validation Failed

end
llm_response:
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant MA as Mobile App
    participant BFF
    participant DB as Database
    participant ES as Email Service

    U->>MA: Fill registration form
    activate MA
    MA->>BFF: POST /register(params)
    activate BFF
    BFF->>BFF: Validate data
    alt Data valid
        BFF->>DB: SELECT user WHERE email = ?
        activate DB
        DB-->>BFF: Result
        deactivate DB
        alt User not exists
            BFF->>DB: INSERT user
            activate DB
            DB-->>BFF: Insert success
            deactivate DB
            BFF->>ES: POST /sendVerification(email)
            activate ES
            ES-->>BFF: Email queued
            deactivate ES
            BFF-->>MA: 201 Created
            deactivate BFF
            MA-->>U: Show "Check email"
            deactivate MA

            Note over U: User clicks verification link
            U->>MA: Open verification link
            activate MA
            MA->>BFF: POST /verify(token)
            activate BFF
            BFF->>DB: UPDATE user SET verified = true
            activate DB
            DB-->>BFF: Update success
            deactivate DB
            BFF-->>MA: 200 OK
            deactivate BFF
            MA-->>U: Show success message
            deactivate MA
        else User exists
            BFF-->>MA: 409 Conflict
            deactivate BFF
            MA-->>U: Show "Email already registered"
            deactivate MA
        end
    else Data invalid
        BFF-->>MA: 400 Bad Request
        deactivate BFF
        MA-->>U: Show validation errors
        deactivate MA
    end
```

The `alt/else/end` blocks are correctly nested and closed. Activations and deactivations are mostly well‑paired; the only minor issue is that `BFF` is deactivated inside the “User not exists” branch before the nested verification-link flow, then reactivated later without an explicit `activate` immediately after the `Note over U`, which can make the activation timeline slightly confusing but is still syntactically acceptable. Overall, it is structurally and syntactically correct MermaidJS with only minor stylistic/clarity concerns.

llm_judge_logic_response: 0.900; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and participants are ordered correctly. All key interactions from the prompt are covered: registration submission, validation, DB insert, email sending, verification link click, account activation, and success/error messages. Minor deviations from the reference (e.g., EmailService responding to BFF instead of User, and not explicitly modeling email delivery) are acceptable as a logically consistent alternative. Overall, the diagram is clear, complete, and structurally sound, with only small stylistic differences from the expected flow.
llm_judge_completeness_response: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and participants match the prompt (User, Mobile App, BFF, Database, Email Service). Requests and responses are consistently paired, with proper use of `->>` and `-->>`, and activations/deactivations are mostly correct and balanced. The main decision points are covered: data validation (valid vs invalid) and, within the valid path, user existence (user not exists vs user exists). The successful flow includes registration, DB insert, sending verification email, user clicking the link, account activation, and success notification. Error/alternate flows include invalid data (400) and existing user (409) with appropriate user-facing messages. Minor differences from the reference (e.g., explicit SELECT before INSERT, BFF receiving email-queued response instead of EmailService->User) are acceptable and still consistent with the specification. The only small issue is that the Email Service does not directly interact with the User in the diagram, whereas the narrative implies sending an email to the user, but this is a minor omission rather than a logical gap. Overall coverage and structure are very good.
llm_judge_activation_handling_response: 0.850; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and activations are mostly used correctly: each `activate` has a matching `deactivate`, and there are no `deactivate` calls without a prior `activate`. Control of execution is generally clear (MA, BFF, DB, ES are activated when they receive work and deactivated after responding), and this does help readability and traceability. Minor issues: (1) BFF is deactivated inside the “User not exists” branch immediately after `201 Created`, then re‑activated later for verification; while legal, this on/off pattern could be simplified by keeping BFF active through the whole registration flow. (2) MA is deactivated and later re‑activated around the verification note; again valid but slightly noisy. Overall, activations are consistent and helpful, with only small stylistic improvements possible.
llm_judge_error_and_status_tracking_resp: 0.900; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and uses participants, alt/else blocks, notes, and activation/deactivation correctly. Status updates are explicit via HTTP status codes (201 Created, 200 OK, 400 Bad Request, 409 Conflict) and user-facing messages (“Show 'Check email'”, “Show success message”, etc.). Success and failure flows are clearly separated: main alt on “Data valid” vs “Data invalid”, and nested alt for “User not exists” vs “User exists”. Error handling is effective, covering invalid data and duplicate user cases with appropriate responses and UI feedback. State changes of key entities are mostly clear (user creation, verification update), though they are implicit in DB operations rather than described as explicit state notes; overall state tracking is still understandable and coherent.

```sequenceDiagram
participant User
participant MobileApp
participant BFF
participant Database
participant EmailService

activate MobileApp
MobileApp->>BFF: POST /register(userData)
activate BFF

activate Database

deactivate Database

    BFF-->>MobileApp: 409 Conflict (Email already registered)

    activate Database

    deactivate Database

    activate EmailService

    deactivate EmailService

    deactivate BFF
    MobileApp-->>User: Show message: Check email for verification
    
    Note over User: User clicks verification link in email

    MobileApp->>BFF: POST /verify(token)
    activate BFF
    BFF->>Database: GET /users?verificationToken={token}
    activate Database
    Database-->>BFF: User found / Not found
    deactivate Database
    
    alt Invalid token
        BFF-->>MobileApp: 400 Bad Request (Invalid token)
        MobileApp-->>User: Show error: Invalid verification link
    else Valid token
        BFF->>Database: PATCH /users/{userId} (activate: true)
        activate Database
        Database-->>BFF: 200 OK (user activated)
        deactivate Database
        
        BFF-->>MobileApp: 200 OK (Account activated)
        deactivate BFF
        MobileApp-->>User: Show success: Account created and verified
    end
end
deactivate MobileApp
```

nl_task_title: Online payment processing for an e-commerce application 7/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Online payment processing for an e-commerce application
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Payment Gateway, Database
Interactions:
1. Product Selection: The user selects products and proceeds to checkout on the mobile app.
2. Payment Request: The mobile app sends the payment details (e.g., amount, payment method) to the BFF.
3. Payment Gateway Interaction: The BFF forwards the payment details to the Payment Gateway for processing.
4. Payment Authorization: The Payment Gateway authorizes the payment and returns a transaction ID to the BFF.
5. Transaction Confirmation: The BFF updates the transaction status in the database and sends a confirmation to the mobile app.
6. Order Confirmation: The mobile app displays an order confirmation message to the user.
7. Error Handling: If the payment fails, the Payment Gateway returns an error code, and the BFF notifies the mobile app to display an error message.
input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```
